content.text: Recall vs Latency in Approximate Nearest-Neighbour Search

Every approximate nearest-neighbour (ANN) index makes a trade-off: you can get exact results but scan the whole corpus, or you can skip most of it and accept that a few true neighbours escape the search.  The two numbers that quantify this trade-off are
recall@K
-- how many of the true top-K neighbours the index actually returns -- and
query latency

-- how long a single lookup takes.  Understanding, measuring, and consciously choosing an operating point on the recall-latency curve is one of the most practically important skills in production vector search.

This sub-subtopic covers the mathematics of both quantities, the mechanisms by which each ANN algorithm moves along its own Pareto frontier, how to benchmark and visualise the trade-off, and how to pick an operating point that satisfies real service-level agreements.
Recall@K -- Definition and Formula
Recall@K is measured on a held-out evaluation set of
Q
queries for each of which the true nearest neighbours are known (usually found by exact brute-force search):

A value of 1.0 means the index always returns the exact top-K.  A value of 0.9 means, on average, 9 of every 10 returned neighbours are true top-K members; one is a false positive that displaced a true neighbour which was missed.
The most common variants in practice are
Recall@1
(does the index return the single nearest neighbour?) and
Recall@10
or
Recall@100
for retrieval tasks.  Semantic search typically targets Recall@10; recommendation systems often use Recall@100 or Recall@1000.
Why recall, not precision?

The set returned by ANN and the set from exact search both have size K, so recall and precision are identical in this setting: every returned vector is either a true positive or displaces a true neighbour.  People say 'recall' because it emphasises what was missed.
Query Latency -- p50, p99, and Throughput
Latency is measured as wall-clock time from query submission to result delivery.  Because latency distributions are right-skewed, two percentile metrics matter:
p50 (median):
half of queries finish faster than this.  Driven by the typical search-path length.
99% of queries finish faster than this.  Driven by worst-case graph traversal, memory stalls, or lock contention.  In SLA conversations, p99 is often the binding constraint.
Throughput

(queries per second, QPS) is a separate but related concept.  Latency is the time for one query; throughput measures how many can be handled simultaneously across all CPU/GPU threads.  The two conflict when an index is CPU-bound: batching queries raises throughput by amortising overhead, but increases per-query latency because a query waits for its batch to fill.  Always report both when characterising performance.
The Pareto Frontier

For any fixed ANN index, varying a single query-time parameter traces a curve in recall-latency space.  No point dominates all others: moving right (higher recall) always costs more latency; moving left saves latency but drops recall.  This curve is called the
Pareto frontier
of the index.

attrs.alt: Recall@10 vs p50 latency Pareto curves for HNSW (M=32) and IVF (nlist=1024), showing operating points ef=16/32/64/128, SLA latency boundary, minimum-recall target, and the feasible operating zone

content.text: Key observations from the diagram above:
Both curves are convex -- each step of recall improvement requires a larger latency increase than the previous step.
HNSW (solid blue) dominates IVF (dashed orange) across most operating points at these index sizes: the same recall is achievable at lower latency.
The sweet spot (ef=64 for HNSW) sits inside the feasible zone where both the SLA latency ceiling and minimum-recall floor are simultaneously satisfied.
Points outside the zone require either accepting lower recall or violating the latency SLA.
How Parameters Move Along the Curve
HNSW -- ef (search beam width)
During a query, HNSW maintains a candidate priority queue of size
ef
.  A larger
ef
expands the beam: more nodes are evaluated at each layer, the graph is explored more thoroughly, and recall rises.  Latency scales roughly linearly with
ef
above a minimum floor set by the graph structure.
where

is the mean graph degree (approximately M in HNSW terminology) and L is the number of layers visited (O(log N)).  Doubling ef roughly doubles latency but does not double recall -- each additional evaluation has diminishing returns as the true neighbours are already in the queue.
IVF -- nprobe (number of cells to search)
IVF clusters the corpus into
nlist
Voronoi cells and, at query time, searches only the closest
nprobe
cells.  The latency cost is proportional to the fraction of the corpus scanned:
Recall improves as
nprobe
rises because vectors near cell boundaries are found when the neighbouring cell is also probed.  Diminishing returns set in because the truly nearest vectors are concentrated in the closest cell(s).
Index-time parameters: M, ef_construction, nlist
These parameters shift the entire frontier rather than tracing along it:
Parameter
Index
Effect on frontier
Build-time cost
M
HNSW
Higher M shifts the curve up-left (better recall for same latency), but each node has more edges to traverse
O(N x M x log N)
ef_construction
HNSW
Higher value improves graph quality; marginal gains beyond ~200
O(N x ef_construction x log N)
nlist
IVF
More cells create tighter clusters; raises the recall ceiling but each cell is smaller so nprobe=1 finds fewer neighbours
k-means: O(N x nlist x d x iters)
Measuring the Trade-off -- Benchmark Protocol
A robust benchmark produces a set of (recall, latency) pairs for each combination of index and query parameter.  The procedure:
Build the index on the full corpus with candidate index-time parameters.
Draw 1,000-10,000 evaluation queries (never overlap with the indexed corpus).
Compute ground truth with brute-force exact search (numpy or faiss IndexFlatL2).
For each query-time parameter value (e.g. ef in {16, 32, 64, 128, 256}), run all eval queries, record p50 and p99 latency, and compute Recall@K.
Plot the resulting (recall, latency) pairs.
Repeat for every index type to compare their frontiers.

import numpy as np
import faiss
import time

def benchmark(index, queries, ground_truth, k=10, ef_values=None, index_type='hnsw'):
    """
    queries      : (Q, d) float32 array
    ground_truth : (Q, k) int64 array of exact neighbour IDs
    ef_values    : list of ints for efSearch (HNSW) or nprobe (IVF)
    """
    Q = queries.shape[0]
    results = []

    for param_val in ef_values:
        if index_type == 'hnsw':
            faiss.downcast_index(index).hnsw.efSearch = param_val
        else:
            faiss.downcast_index(index).nprobe = param_val

        # Warm-up run to populate CPU caches
        index.search(queries[:10], k)

        latencies, all_indices = [], []
        for q in queries:
            t0 = time.perf_counter()
            _, I = index.search(q[None, :], k)
            latencies.append((time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000)  # ms
            all_indices.append(I[0])

        all_indices = np.array(all_indices)  # (Q, k)
        recall = np.mean([
            len(np.intersect1d(all_indices[i], ground_truth[i])) / k
            for i in range(Q)
        ])

        results.append({
            'param': param_val,
            'recall_at_k': round(recall, 4),
            'p50_ms': round(float(np.percentile(latencies, 50)), 2),
            'p99_ms': round(float(np.percentile(latencies, 99)), 2),
        })

    return results

# HNSW example
rows = benchmark(hnsw_index, eval_queries, exact_ids, k=10,
                 ef_values=[16, 32, 64, 128, 256], index_type='hnsw')
for r in rows:
    print(f"ef={r['param']:>4d}  recall={r['recall_at_k']:.4f}  "
          f"p50={r['p50_ms']:.2f}ms  p99={r['p99_ms']:.2f}ms")
Sample output from a 1M-vector benchmark (d=128, M=32):
ef
Recall@10
p50 (ms)
p99 (ms)
QPS (1 thread)
At ef=64 (*), the index is inside the SLA window (p99 <= 3 ms) with recall above 0.97.  ef=128 exceeds the p99 budget.
Choosing an Operating Point
Pick an operating point in three steps:
State the hard constraints.
Identify the latency SLA (p99 <= X ms) and the minimum acceptable recall (Recall@K >= Y).  These come from product requirements, not technical preferences.
Find the feasible zone.
On the benchmarked Pareto curve, the feasible zone is where both constraints are simultaneously satisfied.  If no point is feasible, the index or hardware must change.
Pick the highest-recall feasible point.
Within the feasible zone, choose the point with the highest recall -- you are leaving unused latency headroom, which costs nothing in production.

Common mistake: choosing an operating point based on recall alone on a development machine, then deploying on hardware with different memory bandwidth.  Always benchmark on production-equivalent hardware.
Throughput vs Latency -- A Different Constraint

Latency is not the same as throughput.  At a given operating point, adding CPU threads increases throughput (more queries per second) without changing per-query latency -- up to the point where threads compete for memory bandwidth or cross NUMA boundaries.
The planning formula for a service at QPS target Q with per-query latency L seconds and T threads:

For QPS=1000 and L=1ms (0.001s), you need only one thread.  For QPS=1000 and L=5ms (0.005s), you need five threads.  This formula tells you whether a latency operating point is affordable at a given throughput target.

In memory-bandwidth-limited settings (large d, many concurrent threads), recall can degrade under high concurrency because cache misses increase.  Always benchmark at production concurrency, not single-threaded.
Re-ranking as a Recall-Latency Decoupling Mechanism
Re-ranking is a two-stage strategy that decouples the recall-latency trade-off:
ANN search retrieves a candidate set of size K' >> K at low ef (fast, moderate recall).
A second exact pass re-scores the K' candidates using the original full-precision vectors.
Return the true top-K from the re-scored candidates.
Because K' candidates are retrieved cheaply and exact scoring is done only on a small set, the overall recall can exceed what the ANN index alone achieves at any latency-feasible ef.

import faiss
import numpy as np

def two_stage_search(hnsw_index, corpus_vectors, query, k=10, k_prime=100, ef_search=32):
    """
    Stage 1: ANN retrieves k_prime candidates quickly.
    Stage 2: Exact re-scoring on those candidates only.
    """
    faiss.downcast_index(hnsw_index).hnsw.efSearch = ef_search
    _, candidate_ids = hnsw_index.search(query[None, :], k_prime)  # (1, k_prime)
    candidate_ids = candidate_ids[0]

    # Exact L2 distances on the small candidate pool
    candidates = corpus_vectors[candidate_ids]  # (k_prime, d)
    dists = np.linalg.norm(candidates - query, axis=1)  # (k_prime,)
    top_k_local = np.argsort(dists)[:k]
    return candidate_ids[top_k_local]

# With ef=32 (fast) + re-ranking from 100 candidates:
# recall climbs from ~0.944 to ~0.991 -- near ef=256 quality at ef=32 speed.

Re-ranking cost is proportional to K' x d (dot products or L2), which is typically negligible compared to graph traversal.  The net effect shifts the Pareto frontier up and to the left -- better recall without proportional latency increase.
Algorithm Comparison at Equivalent Operating Points
Different algorithms occupy different parts of the recall-latency space even after tuning:
Algorithm
Recall@10 at 1ms p50
Recall@10 at 5ms p50
Memory/vector
Build time
Brute-force (exact)
1.000 (trivially)
4d bytes
HNSW M=32
~4d + M*8 bytes
slow
IVF nlist=1024
4d bytes + inverted lists
fast
IVF-PQ m=8
m bytes + lists
fast
HNSW + re-rank K'=100
4d + M*8 bytes
slow
Numbers above are indicative for 1M SIFT128 vectors on a modern CPU; results vary with dimensionality, data distribution, and hardware.  Always benchmark on your own data.
Production Monitoring and Drift
After deployment, the recall-latency operating point can drift due to:
Corpus growth:
adding vectors shifts the distribution and may increase traversal depth.  Rebuild or update the index periodically.
Query distribution shift:
if user queries move to a different region of embedding space, previously calibrated ef may be insufficient.
Memory pressure:
as the index grows, more of it falls out of L3 cache, increasing latency at the same ef.

Monitor recall continuously by running your ground-truth eval set on a schedule -- not just at deployment time -- and alert when recall drops below the target floor.  Monitoring latency alone is insufficient; an index can be fast because it is wrong.
ANN-Benchmarks -- Standard Evaluation Framework

ANN-Benchmarks is the standard reproducible benchmarking harness for comparing ANN libraries.  It runs a Docker container per library, executes a standard protocol, and produces recall-latency plots for each dataset.
Standard benchmark datasets:
SIFT1M:
1M 128-dim float32 vectors (visual descriptors). Oldest and most commonly cited.
GIST1M:
1M 960-dim vectors -- tests high-dimensionality behaviour.
GloVe-100:
1.2M 100-dim text embeddings -- inner-product distance.
Fashion-MNIST:
60K 784-dim image embeddings -- useful for small-scale tests.

Key insight from ANN-Benchmarks: no single algorithm dominates across all datasets.  HNSW tends to win on high-recall, low-latency benchmarks.  IVF-PQ wins on memory-constrained deployments with billions of vectors.  Benchmarking on your own data and hardware is irreplaceable.
Common Mistakes
Using index defaults in production.
Default ef=10 or nprobe=1 often gives recall below 0.7.  Always benchmark and set parameters explicitly.
Benchmarking at wrong concurrency.
Single-threaded latency underestimates real latency under load.  Measure at production QPS.
Treating recall as a build-time property.
Recall depends on query-time parameters; the same index can give 0.7 or 0.999 depending on ef.
Ignoring tail latency.
p50 can be within SLA while p99 blows through it.  Both must pass.
Not measuring recall separately from end-to-end quality.
Application-level metrics (NDCG, MRR) can mask recall loss if re-ranking compensates.  Know both.
Practical Configuration Guide
Below are starting-point configurations for common deployment scenarios.  Treat these as baselines to benchmark from, not final answers.
Scenario
Index
Index-time params
Query-time start
Typical Recall@10
< 1M vectors, strict SLA (p99 < 2ms)
HNSW
M=32, ef_construction=200
ef=32
< 1M vectors, relaxed SLA (p99 < 10ms)
HNSW
M=48, ef_construction=400
10M vectors, memory-constrained
IVF-PQ
nlist=4096, m=16
nprobe=64
100M+ vectors, GPU cluster
IVF-PQ
nprobe=256
Any size, highest recall required
HNSW + re-rank
M=32, ef_construction=200
For the IVF family, a rule of thumb for choosing
nlist
is to set it to approximately the square root of the corpus size:

This balances the centroid-search cost (proportional to nlist) against the per-cell scan cost (proportional to N/nlist).  With nlist = sqrt(N), both costs are O(sqrt(N)) and an nprobe that covers roughly sqrt(N) cells corresponds to scanning ~1/sqrt(N) of the corpus.

For HNSW, the M parameter controls average graph degree.  Higher M improves recall at any given ef, but increases index size (each node stores M outgoing edges as int32) and build time.  M=16 is a common memory-efficient choice; M=32 or M=64 are typical high-recall configurations.

import faiss
import numpy as np

def build_hnsw(corpus, M=32, ef_construction=200):
    d = corpus.shape[1]
    index = faiss.IndexHNSWFlat(d, M)
    index.hnsw.efConstruction = ef_construction
    index.add(corpus.astype('float32'))
    return index

def build_ivf_flat(corpus, nlist=None):
    d = corpus.shape[1]
    N = corpus.shape[0]
    if nlist is None:
        nlist = int(np.sqrt(N))  # rule-of-thumb
    quantiser = faiss.IndexFlatL2(d)
    index = faiss.IndexIVFFlat(quantiser, d, nlist)
    index.train(corpus.astype('float32'))
    index.add(corpus.astype('float32'))
    return index

def build_ivf_pq(corpus, nlist=None, m=8, nbits=8):
    d = corpus.shape[1]
    N = corpus.shape[0]
    if nlist is None:
        nlist = int(np.sqrt(N))
    assert d % m == 0, 'Dimension must be divisible by m'
    quantiser = faiss.IndexFlatL2(d)
    index = faiss.IndexIVFPQ(quantiser, d, nlist, m, nbits)
    index.train(corpus.astype('float32'))
    index.add(corpus.astype('float32'))
    return index

# 1M vectors, d=128
N, d = 1_000_000, 128
corpus = np.random.randn(N, d).astype('float32')

hnsw = build_hnsw(corpus, M=32, ef_construction=200)
ivf  = build_ivf_flat(corpus, nlist=int(np.sqrt(N)))
ivfpq = build_ivf_pq(corpus, nlist=int(np.sqrt(N)), m=16)

print(f'HNSW size : {hnsw.sa_encode(corpus[:1]).nbytes * N / 1e9:.1f} GB (approx)')
print(f'IVF-PQ size: ~{N * 16 / 1e9:.2f} GB (m=16 bytes/vector)')
Recall Under Query Distribution Shift

Recall numbers from a benchmark eval set are only valid when the eval queries are representative of production queries.  When they are not -- because the product evolved, because user behaviour changed, or because the model producing embeddings was updated -- the measured recall can be optimistic.
Three signals that distribution shift is occurring:
Recall on the fixed eval set is stable, but downstream quality metrics drop.
The eval set no longer covers the new query distribution.
Average ef-candidate distance (mean distance to the ef-th explored node) rises over time.
Queries are landing in sparser regions of the graph.
Fraction of queries returning fewer than K results increases.
Rare when data is dense, but common after model updates that shift the embedding distribution.
The correct response is to refresh the eval set from recent production queries (using shadow search to collect ground truth) and re-benchmark.  If recall has genuinely degraded, options are:
Raise ef/nprobe (immediate, no rebuild required).
Rebuild the index on the updated corpus with better index-time parameters.
Fine-tune or replace the embedding model and rebuild from scratch.
Periodically refreshing the eval set is therefore not optional -- it is a maintenance task on the same cadence as index rebuilds.
Best Practices
Build a recall-latency plot before committing to an index type. The shape of the curve tells you which algorithm fits your constraints.
Set ef/nprobe to the highest value that satisfies the latency SLA -- never lower.
Use re-ranking to gain 2-4 recall points at low marginal cost when the candidate pool is O(100).
Monitor recall in production on a schedule with a fixed eval set. Alert on drops, not just latency spikes.
Store benchmark results alongside the index configuration -- future engineers need to see the curve, not just the chosen point.
For billion-scale deployments, profile memory bandwidth utilisation before adding CPU threads; beyond a NUMA boundary, latency rises and recall may fall.
Key Takeaways
Recall@K measures the fraction of true top-K neighbours returned; latency measures how long one query takes. They trade off against each other.
Each ANN index traces a Pareto frontier in recall-latency space. A single query-time parameter (ef for HNSW, nprobe for IVF) moves a point along the frontier without rebuilding.
Index-time parameters (M, nlist) shift the entire frontier but require a rebuild.
Choose an operating point by stating latency and recall constraints, finding the feasible zone on the benchmarked curve, and picking the highest-recall feasible point.
Throughput (QPS) is separate from latency; plan threads as T = ceil(QPS x L_seconds).
Re-ranking on a small candidate set is the most cost-effective way to raise recall beyond the Pareto frontier of the ANN index alone.
Monitor recall continuously in production; an index can become fast and wrong as data drifts.
